metadata.title: Starty - multilingual editions
metadata.description:
Explore the Starty comics in a vibrant collection of multilingual editions, created within the Horizon Europe-funded START Project. Designed to make science accessible, engaging, and enjoyable, these comics bring research to life through storytelling and colourful illustrations.

To reach and inspire a truly international audience, the series is available in multiple languages: Danish (DK), Dutch (NL), English (ENG), French (FR), German (GER), Hungarian (HG), Italian (IT), Norwegian (NOR), Polish (PL), Portuguese (PT), Slovak (SK), and Spanish (SP).

By offering these diverse linguistic editions, this collection aims to broaden accessibility, foster inclusivity, and strengthen engagement across a wide and diverse community. Whether you are a researcher, student, educator, policymaker, industry professional, or simply a curious reader, Starty invites you to discover science in a fun, approachable, and globally connected way.

metadata.title: D9.2 Plan for dissemination and exploitation including communication activities
metadata.description:
Deliverable D9.2 “Plan for dissemination and exploitation including communication activities” establishes the strategic and operational roadmap for maximizing the impact, visibility, and long-term sustainability of the UNICHE project. The plan implements a dynamic, layered Communication and Dissemination (C&D) strategy that evolves across four project phases, with the primary goal of fostering a vibrant community of CH and creative professionals.

Key efforts focus on:

  • Targeted Outreach: Adapting messages for diverse groups, including CH professionals, researchers, policymakers, and creative SMEs.

  • Stakeholder Involvement and Validation: Supporting co-creation activities and pilot deployments in diverse settings and executing an open call to third parties to test the platform.

  • Ecosystem Alignment: Ensuring alignment with the ECCCH and its sister projects to promote interoperability and knowledge sharing.

The Exploitation Framework provides the structure for long-term sustainability, centered on the strategic identification and planning of Key Exploitable Results (KERs). The IPR management approach prioritizes open innovation and an open core licensing model for the platform to foster broad sector adoption while securing commercial and non-profit exploitation pathways. The plan details Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to continuously monitor the success of all C&D and exploitation activities in achieving the project’s objective of long-term impact and scalability.

data.title: D9.2 Plan for dissemination and exploitation including communication activities

metadata.title: Cultural Significance and Infrastructure Challenges of Jayanti Devi Temple: A Geographical Perspective
metadata.description:
Sacred places are spaces where one can connect with the divine and engage in religious activities. Sacred landscapes hold cultural, historical and spiritual significance, shaping the geographical and socio-economic fabric of regions. The Jayanti Devi temple, dedicated to the goddess Jayanti, situated on the foothills of the Shivalik ranges in Jayanti Majri village, Punjab, 15 kilometres from Chandigarh, is not just a site of worship rather it is a blend of history, faith and community life. This temple is a perfect example of how a sacred landscape shapes and is shaped by the culture, tradition, beliefs and everyday life. Analysing the information from field survey, interviews and oral narratives, the research revealed that the temple has evolved into a significant source of financial support for the surrounding region. The temple's cultural relevance is kept alive through a 2-day fair organised every year twice in the month of February and August, which transforms it into a vibrant centre of communal identity. Yet, challenges are faced like accessibility, transportation services, inadequate infrastructure and environmental degradation. This research aims to suggest some ways in which the temple’s development can have a balance between tradition and growth.

Key Words:. Sacred Landscape, Religious Tourism, Jyanti Devi Temple, Cultural Significance, Infrastructural Development, Communal Identity.

metadata.title: Bones, shells and baselines—how the past can inform modern marine management, protection and restoration
metadata.description:
In this article, we explore how archaeological and historical perspectives

can inform current marine management, protection and restoration efforts,

and entice us to rethink our present-day relationships with ocean resources

to secure sustainable management. Historical records, archaeological

findings, palaeoecological data, local and traditional ecological knowledge

are coming together in highly interdisciplinary ways, combining cutting-

edge analytical and traditional methods. This research is producing more

detailed and nuanced descriptions of past ecosystems that reveal the

nature, scale and timing of human influences on the sea in unprecedented

detail. The findings help us reset ‘shifted baselines’—a phenomenon

where long-altered ecosystems are perceived as natural. By bringing

clarity to historical human impacts, we can challenge current management

paradigms, raise ambitions for recovery of biodiversity loss, improve

approaches to habitat restoration and identify promising targets for

rewilding. A deeper appreciation of what vibrant seas really look like helps

us value and better communicate the benefits recovery can bring for society.

Understanding ocean responses to past climates helps us predict and

respond to emerging climate pressures today, helping managers develop

better approaches to enhance resilience in the face of ongoing and future

environmental changes.

metadata.title: Retention Strategies for Millennials in the Indian Startup Ecosystem: A Strategic Framework
metadata.description:
Abstract

The Indian startup ecosystem has witnessed unprecedented growth, positioning India as one of the world's largest and most vibrant hubs for entrepreneurial ventures. However, this rapid expansion has brought the challenge of talent retention to the forefront of organizational strategy. Millennials, who now constitute the primary demographic of the Indian workforce, possess distinct expectations regarding professional development, work-life integration, and organizational values that diverge significantly from traditional employment models. When these expectations remain unmet, Indian startups experience high turnover rates, resulting in substantial financial loss, erosion of intellectual capital, and hampered innovation cycles. This study synthesizes empirical research to examine the complex determinants of millennial retention and proposes a strategic framework—centered on Personalized Employee Experience (PEX)—to foster long-term loyalty, cultural alignment, and organizational health within the unique, high-velocity Indian market. This expanded version further explores the role of data-driven HR analytics, the nuances of the evolving psychological contract, the socio-cultural pressures unique to the Indian urban landscape, and the strategic importance of building an "employer brand" that resonates with the modern, purpose-driven workforce.

Keywords:Millennials, Indian Startups, Employee Retention, Talent Management, Organizational Culture, Job Satisfaction, PEX, Psychological Contract, HR Analytics, Workforce Diversity, Gig-Economy, Employer Branding

1. Introduction: The High-Stakes Talent Landscape

The Indian startup landscape is defined by its high-pressure, resource-constrained, and hyper-competitive nature. In major tech corridors like Bengaluru, Gurugram, Hyderabad, and Pune, the "war for talent" is intense, with established multinational corporations (MNCs) and well-funded unicorns vying for the same pool of skilled professionals. For a startup, the cost of losing a key employee involves not just the immediate replacement expense—often estimated at  1.5 to 2  times the annual salary of the departing individual—but also the loss of institutional memory, the disruption of team morale, and the stalling of critical project momentum.

Millennials in India are unique in their desire for autonomy, impact, and purposeful work. Unlike previous generations that may have prioritized long-term job security and steady, predictable progression, the millennial cohort is often motivated by rapid skill acquisition, the ability to contribute to a larger mission, and the flexibility to manage personal lives alongside professional growth. As startups operate in environments of extreme uncertainty and frequent pivots, aligning these intrinsic motivators with the startup’s volatile growth trajectory is the ultimate challenge for founders and HR leaders.

Furthermore, the post-pandemic shifts in professional expectations have accelerated the demand for "human-centric" workplaces. The "psychological contract"—the set of unwritten, mutual expectations between an employee and their employer—has become the invisible adhesive holding these high-stakes organizations together. The objective of this paper is to unpack these dynamics and provide an actionable framework for modern founders.

2. Factors Influencing Millennial Retention

The retention of millennial talent in India relies on a delicate balance of tangible rewards and intangible cultural drivers.

Table 1: Factors Influencing Millennial Retention

Factor

Description

Impact on Retention

Career Development

Access to mentorship, rapid promotion cycles, and upskilling opportunities.

High: Reduces stagnation and builds long-term commitment.

Work-Life Balance

Flexibility in work hours, remote options, and outcome-based performance.

High: Essential for preventing burnout in high-pressure environments.

Work Environment

Supportive peer networks, psychological safety, and flat hierarchies.

Medium: Influences daily job satisfaction and cultural alignment.

Compensation

Competitive base pay, performance bonuses, and ESOPs (Stock Options).

High: Essential for talent acquisition, though secondary to growth in the long run.

Purpose

Aligning the company’s vision with social or personal values.

Medium: Drives deeper engagement and discretionary effort.

The Evolving Psychological Contract

Beyond tangible benefits, the "psychological contract" is a powerful driver of retention. Millennials view their employment as a partnership. When a startup promises a culture of innovation but enforces rigid, legacy-style reporting structures, the resulting dissonance leads to swift disengagement. Retention, therefore, depends on the transparent communication of expectations from Day 1. Every missed promotion promise or failure to provide promised autonomy acts as a significant withdrawal from the "trust bank," leading to "quiet quitting" or resignation.

The Role of Compensation and Wealth Creation

In the Indian context, while initial base salary is critical for survival, long-term retention is increasingly driven by the "wealth creation" narrative. Startups that effectively educate employees on the potential value of their equity—treating them as co-owners—create a sense of long-term alignment that cash-heavy MNCs often struggle to replicate.

3. Strategic Retention Framework: The Personalized Employee Experience (PEX)

To thrive, Indian startups must pivot toward a Personalized Employee Experience (PEX). This model moves away from "one-size-fits-all" HR policies.













Fig.1: Strategic Retention Framework: The Personalized Employee Experience (PEX)

Implementation of PEX

  • Individualized Growth Maps: Startups should implement bi-annual career mapping. This collaborative exercise ensures that the employee sees a clear, tangible path for personal advancement within the organization, rather than feeling they must leave to secure the next step in their career.

  • Flexible Work Ecosystems: In the Indian urban context, where infrastructure challenges like heavy traffic often contribute to exhaustion, PEX-driven startups prioritize output—measured by quality and milestones reached—over the physical duration spent at a desk.

  • Leadership Feedback Loops: Traditional hierarchies often create a high "power distance." Startups must establish informal, frequent, and safe channels for bottom-up communication, such as "Skip-level" meetings and anonymous pulse surveys.

4. The Role of HR Analytics in Retention

Modern retention strategy is increasingly data-driven. Startups must leverage HR analytics to move from reactive to predictive retention management.

  1. Churn Prediction Modeling: Analyzing attendance patterns, engagement scores, and performance metrics to identify employees at risk.

  2. Sentiment Analysis: Using AI-driven surveys to track the "mood" of the organization in real-time.

  3. The "Stay" Interview: Proactively asking high-potential employees what keeps them engaged, rather than waiting for an exit interview to discover the issues.

5. Challenges in the Indian Context

Startups frequently struggle with the "instability trap" and the "global talent marketplace." Indian millennials benchmark their benefits against MNCs. Additionally, the cultural tendency towards parental approval for stable careers creates friction for those choosing the riskier startup path. Startups must build an "employer brand" that resonates with the individual’s social circle, highlighting the long-term potential of equity and the unique skill set acquired through entrepreneurial ventures.

6. Recommendations for Startup Leaders

  • Adopt Transformational Leadership: Shift from being "managers" to "leaders."

  • Integrate Sustainability and Ethics: Implementing Green Human Resource Management (GHRM).

  • Formalize Feedback Mechanisms: Move toward structured, yet low-bureaucracy, feedback cycles.

  • Build Community: Treat the workplace as a community through knowledge-sharing and peer-mentoring.

  • Leverage Data for Retention: Treat retention metrics with the same rigor as product-market fit metrics.

  • Focus on Holistic Wellbeing: Offer access to mental health resources, acknowledging that burnout is a significant risk in the "always-on" startup culture.

Conclusion

The retention of millennial talent in India is a strategic business imperative. By shifting the focus from transactional employment to a relational, personalized experience, founders can build organizations that are not only high-performing but also resilient. The future belongs to organizations that treat talent as their most valuable product.

References

  1. Ghani, B., et al. (2022). Challenges and strategies for employee retention in the hospitality industry. Sustainability.

  2. Setyaningrum, R. P., et al. (2024). Green human resource management and millennial retention. Cogent Business & Management.

metadata.title: Digital Space and Human Life in A Technological Society
metadata.description: The digital space is no longer merely a tool to support physical life; it has become a new environment where people exist, communicate, work, and consume. The emergence of the internet, social media platforms, artificial intelligence, and digital ecosystems has blurred the lines between real and virtual space. In the technocratic era, the digital space is realized as a vibrant dimension where people not only participate passively but also construct their own identities, relationships, and worldviews. Therefore, to build a harmonious society, the author chooses the topic of digital space and human life in a technocratic society as the subject of this research.
keywords: Digital space as an environment of existence, Fragmentation and power building, Redefining community in virtual space, Loss of intimate and personal space, Interaction between physical and digital reality.

journal.title: INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF SOCIAL SCIENCE HUMANITY & MANAGEMENT RESEARCH

data.title: Digital Space and Human Life in A Technological Society

metadata.title: The Toy Effect in Packaging Aimed at Children
metadata.description:
In contemporary consumer culture, children are no longer viewed merely as end users of products; they are now active participants who directly influence purchasing decisions. This shift has significantly increased the importance of packaging design in products aimed at children. Packaging has evolved beyond its traditional functions of protection and information delivery to become a powerful visual communication tool that attracts attention, entertains, and establishes emotional connections. In particular, children’s packaging designs that incorporate toy-like characteristics are discussed in the literature within the framework of concepts such as the “toy effect” or “toyification.”

Given children’s cognitive and perceptual development, it is evident that colorful, interactive, and play-oriented visual stimuli exert a strong psychological influence on them. According to Piaget’s theory of cognitive development, children understand and interpret objects primarily through play, a fundamental mechanism for learning and meaning-making. Similarly, Vygotsky emphasizes the crucial role of play in children’s learning processes and social interactions, highlighting its contribution to cognitive and emotional development. Within this theoretical context, packaging designs that embody toy-like qualities encourage children to perceive products not merely as consumable items but as objects of play and entertainment.

The toy effect in packaging design is manifested through elements such as three-dimensional forms, rounded and soft shapes, vibrant color palettes, character-based illustrations, and interactive structures that invite manipulation and reuse. These features enhance children’s curiosity and engagement, fostering an emotional attachment to both the packaging and the product. As a result, packaging may gain a symbolic value independent of the product it contains, sometimes becoming more appealing to children than the product itself. This phenomenon highlights the growing influence of packaging as an experiential and playful medium within children’s consumer environments.

From a consumption perspective, toy-like packaging designs play a significant role in shaping children’s preferences and desires. Such designs not only increase product visibility on store shelves but also contribute to early brand recognition and loyalty. Children exposed to playful, entertaining packaging may develop positive attitudes toward specific brands at an early age, which can influence their future consumption behavior. Furthermore, the toy effect often extends beyond the point of purchase, as children may continue to use the packaging as a play object, thereby prolonging interaction with the brand.

However, the increasing use of the toy effect in children’s packaging design also raises ethical concerns. Given children’s limited cognitive ability to distinguish between persuasive intent and playful presentation, excessively toy-like packaging may lead to manipulative consumption practices. This is particularly problematic in health-related products, such as food items, where playful packaging can overshadow nutritional information and mislead young consumers. Therefore, the responsibility of designers and manufacturers becomes critical in balancing entertainment value with ethical considerations.

This study aims to examine how the relationships among play, entertainment, and consumption shape the toy effect in children’s packaging design. By analyzing the perceptual and psychological effects of toy-like packaging on children, the research examines both its communicative power and its ethical implications. Ultimately, the study emphasizes the need to adopt a responsible, child-centered design approach that considers children’s developmental needs while avoiding exploitative consumption strategies.
keywords: Children's packaging design, toy influence, visual communication, consumer behavior, play, and entertainment.
references: Ambrose, G., & Harris, P. (2011). Packaging the brand: The relationship between packaging design and brand identity. AVA Publishing.

metadata.title: Emotional Cubism: Synthesizing Cubism and the Art of Van Gogh
metadata.description:
Emotional Cubism is a contemporary art movement that fuses the geometric multiplicity of Cubism with the expressive, color-driven intensity of Vincent van Gogh’s oeuvre. By combining multiple perspectives, fractured forms, and vibrant emotional color, this hybrid style seeks to create works that are intellectually complex yet deeply affective. This paper explores the conceptual underpinnings, aesthetic strategies, and cultural significance of Emotional Cubism, situating it within both historical and postmodern contexts.



Originality and AI-use statement:
This work is an original research output by Begüm Yıldırım. AI tools, if used, were limited to language refinement, grammar correction, formatting, translation assistance, and clarity improvement. The conceptual framework, research direction, interpretation, models, and conclusions belong to the author. External sources, datasets, or prior works are cited where applicable.

data.title: Emotional Cubism: Synthesizing Cubism and the Art of Van Gogh

metadata.title: Zavaidoc – A Legend of Interwar Bucharest
metadata.description: The novel Zavaidoc în anul iubirii by Doina Ruști, published in 2024 by Bookzone, offers a hypnotic foray into interwar Bucharest – a vibrant and enigmatic city where the chords of the fiddle blend harmoniously with the hidden secrets of both slums and bourgeois salons. The central figure, though not a traditional protagonist, Zavaidoc, is transformed from a historical character into a mythical archetype. His voice is more than mere talent; it becomes an almost magical force, capable of bewitching, seducing, or disturbing the spirit. Doina Ruști turns the legendary Zavaidoc into a catalyst for passionate events, placing him at the center of a rich and complex narrative.

journal.title: Patrimoniul cultural de ieri – implicații în dezvoltarea societății de mâine

data.title: Zavaidoc – A Legend of Interwar Bucharest
